coredata.dc:title: Enhanced graphene quantum dot fluorescence nanosensor for highly sensitive acetylcholinesterase assay and inhibitor screening
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Tian
Qi, Liang
Zhang, Jing
Huang, Ya-Li
Zhang, Zhi-Qi
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive nanosensor based on the enhanced fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by 3-(ethyliminomethyleneamino)-N,N-dimethyl-propan-1-amine/N-hydroxysuccinimide was constructed to determine acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity and to screen for its inhibitors. The approach relied on the fact that the enhanced fluorescence of GQDs could be effectively quenched by Hg2+, and that thiol compounds released by acetylthiocholine iodide (ATCh) under AChE catalytic hydrolysis could interact with Hg2+ through the formation of Hg-S bonds, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of GQDs. The fluorescence recovery ratio of GQDs was proportional to the dose of AChE, and an analytical method for detecting AChE was constructed with a limit of detection of 2.3×10− 6 U. A model for AChE inhibition was further established and two traditional AChE inhibitors were employed to verify the feasibility of the system. Because of the enhanced fluorescence and lower amount of GQDs, lower IC50 values of 63.76 and 14.07nM were obtained for paraoxon and tacrine, respectively. The developed protocol provides a new and promising platform for assaying AChE activity and screening its inhibitors with low cost and high sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence
Hg2+
Acetylcholinesterase
Inhibitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of AChE activity with enhanced GQD fluorescence
Influence of inhibitors on AChE activity
Effect of EDC/NHS dose on fluorescence enhancement
Optimal conditions for the AChE assay
The quenching effect of Hg2+ on the enhanced GQD fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the assay strategy for the detection of acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) GQDs (25μgmL−1) with (dashed line) and without (solid line) 2.5mM EDC/NHS, (b) GQDs (5μgmL−1) in the assay solution containing various concentrations of EDC/NHS (0.5, 1, 2, 2.5, 4 and 5mM) and (c) GQDs (2.5, 5, 10, 15, 25μgmL−1) in the presence of 2.5mM EDC/NHS in the assay solution. λ ex =387nm, λ em =457nm.

Quenching effect of the fluorescence of (a) the GQDs (5μgmL−1) containing EDC/NHS (2.5mM) by various concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 10, 25, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100μM) (inset: the dynamic ranges) and (b) the GQDs (1mgmL−1) by various concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500, 700μM) (inset: the dynamic ranges).

Fluorescence recovery of GQDs containing EDC/NHS and AChE by addition of ATCh after quenching by Hg2+ (insets: the dynamic ranges). The doses of GQDs, EDC/NHS, Hg2+, and AChE are 5μgmL−1, 2.5mM, 50μM, and 0.05U, respectively. λ ex =387nm, λ em =457nm. Curves 1–7: curve 8 with addition of Hg2+ and final ATCh concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 100μM, respectively; curve 8: GQDs containing EDC/NHS and AChE.

Fluorescence recovery of GQDs containing EDC/NHS and ATCh by adding varying doses of AChE after quenching by Hg2+ (insets: the dynamic ranges). The doses of GQDs, EDC/NHS, Hg2+, and ATCh are 5μgmL−1, 2.5mM, 50μM, and 500μM, respectively. λ ex =387nm, λ em =457nm. Curve 1: GQDs containing EDC/NHS, ATCh, and Hg2+; curves 2–8: curve 1 with final AChE concentrations of 0.0001, 0.0004, 0.0006, 0.0008, 0.001, 0.0013, and 0.0018U, respectively; curve 9: GQDs containing EDC/NHS and ATCh.

Emission spectra of GQD system in the presence of various concentrations of (a) paraoxon and (b) tacrine, and the inhibition ratios (insets). The doses of GQDs, EDC/NHS, Hg2+, AChE, and ATCh are 5μgmL−1, 2.5mM, 50μM, 0.05U, and 50μM, respectively. λ ex =387nm, λ em =457nm. (a) Curve 1: GQDs containing EDC/NHS and AChE; curves 2–8: GQDs containing EDC/NHS, AChE, Hg2+ and ATCh with final paraoxon concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, and 500nM, respectively; curve 9: GQDs containing EDC/NHS, AChE, and Hg2+. (b) Curve 1: GQDs containing EDC/NHS and AChE; curves 2–8: GQDs containing EDC/NHS, AChE, Hg2+ and ATCh with final tacrine concentrations of 0, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40, and 60nM, respectively; curve 9: GQDs containing EDC/NHS, AChE, and Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Enhanced graphene quantum dot fluorescence nanosensor for highly sensitive acetylcholinesterase assay and inhibitor screening

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province; Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resource and Natural Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Ministry of Education; School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an 710062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province; Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resource and Natural Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Ministry of Education; School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive nanosensor based on the enhanced fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by 3-(ethyliminomethyleneamino)-N,N-dimethyl-propan-1-amine/N-hydroxysuccinimide was constructed to determine acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity and to screen for its inhibitors. The approach relied on the fact that the enhanced fluorescence of GQDs could be effectively quenched by Hg2+, and that thiol compounds released by acetylthiocholine iodide (ATCh) under AChE catalytic hydrolysis could interact with Hg2+ through the formation of Hg-S bonds, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of GQDs. The fluorescence recovery ratio of GQDs was proportional to the dose of AChE, and an analytical method for detecting AChE was constructed with a limit of detection of 2.3×10− 6 U. A model for AChE inhibition was further established and two traditional AChE inhibitors were employed to verify the feasibility of the system. Because of the enhanced fluorescence and lower amount of GQDs, lower IC50 values of 63.76 and 14.07nM were obtained for paraoxon and tacrine, respectively. The developed protocol provides a new and promising platform for assaying AChE activity and screening its inhibitors with low cost and high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials, widely known for their different morphologies like zero-dimensional fullerene [1], one-dimensional carbon nanotubes [2,3], and two-dimensional graphene [4,5], have been used in the biological, chemical, material, and environmental fields over the past few decades [6].

As a class of zero-dimensional graphitic nanomaterials with lateral dimensions of less than 100nm in a single layer, double layers, and multi-layers (3 to <10) [7,8], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibiting fluorescence have recently aroused increasing interest in their optical and electronic properties [9]. Quantum confinement, edge effects, oxygen-containing groups, structural defects, and doping elements contribute considerably to the luminescent properties of the GQDs [10,11]. With excellent characteristics like large surface area and diameter, fine surface grafting using the π–π conjugated network, and other special physical properties, GQDs are superior because of their chemical inertness, ease of production, resistance to photobleaching, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility in comparison with conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), thus making GQDs promising in sensors, bioimaging applications, and optoelectronic devices [12]. In addition, the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at their edge enable them to display excellent water solubility and render them suitable for successive functionalization with various organic, inorganic, polymeric, or biological species [13,14]. GQDs are increasingly considered to be a promising material to replace the commonly used semiconductor for biosensing and bioimaging related applications [15], detection of uric acid [16] and sensing for metal ions [17,18].

Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) is a type of serine esterase in the animal central nervous system [19]. It mainly regulates the levels of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine by catalyzing the hydrolysis reaction of acetylcholine (ACh) to choline. Many neurodegenerative diseases (Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, etc.) are associated with the degeneration of the cholinergic system, resulting in a decrease in the amount of neurotransmitters such as acetylcholine [20]. Inhibition of AChE hydrolysis increases the ACh concentration and provides an alternative to symptomatic treatment. AChE inhibitors that penetrate the blood-brain barrier, such as donepezil, rivastigmine, galanthamine, and tacrine, have been proven to be useful in the symptomatic treatment of neurodegenerative diseases [21]. Assays for AChE inhibition are important in a broad range of fields, including health care, pharmaceuticals, food and agriculture, environment monitoring, and quality control. In recent years, a number of fluorescent, colorimetric, electrochemical, and advanced materials such as modified electrodes [22], polymers [23,24], graphene oxide nanosheets [25], carbon nanotubes [26], and gold nanoparticles [20] have also been used for the construction of novel AChE assay strategies. Undoubtedly, the most widely used technique of AChE assay is Ellman's colorimetric method [27].

Hg2+ at low concentrations can quench the fluorescence of GQDs [28]. Biothiol compound (GSH, Cys, or Hcy) can selectively bind to Hg2+ through Hg-S bonding interactions. In turn, the Hg2+-GQDs complex is dissociated, and a turn-on fluorescence signal can be detected [29]. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs could be significantly enhanced with 3-(ethyliminomethyleneamino)-N,N-dimethyl-propan-1-amine (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) at the same concentration (EDC/NHS) in an aqueous MES-Tris buffer solution. Herein we report an enhanced GQD fluorescence-based method for highly sensitive sensing of AChE activity and screening of its potential inhibitors. As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescence of GQDs was enhanced with EDC/NHS in the enzyme immobilization procedure. After quenching the fluorescence with the addition of Hg2+, acetylthiocholine iodide (ATCh) was added as it could be hydrolyzed by AChE to yield thiocholine, which interacted with Hg2+ to recover the quenched fluorescence. The fluorescence recovery level could be directly related to the amount of thiocholine released into the assay solution. Finally, a highly sensitive assay method for AChE was constructed based on this process. With the addition of an inhibitor, the enzyme's activity decreased, and the fluorescence recovery could be restricted. The study successfully demonstrates that the enhanced GQD system could be employed for the detection of AChE activity and screening of AChE inhibitors.

AChE (EC 3.1.1.7) from Electrophorus electricus (200–1000U/mg) and ATCh were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). GQDs (1mgmL−1) were purchased from XF NANO Inc. (Nanjing, China). EDC/NHS, MES monohydrate acid, and Tris buffer solution were obtained from MP Biomedicals LLC (Illkirch, France). HgCl2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultra-pure water (18.2MΩcm) was produced by a Millipore purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used to prepare all aqueous solutions. The UV–vis adsorption spectra were measured on a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Puxi Analytic Instrument Ltd. of Beijing, China). Fluorescence measurements were performed on a LS-55 Luminescence Spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer Company, USA) with a 300μL quartz cuvette. The slit width is 5nm both for excitation and emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AChE activity with enhanced GQD fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: AChE was immobilized onto GQDs based on the reported mechanism [30] with slight modification. In brief, GQDs were added into MES-Tris buffer solution (20mM, pH 6.2) with AChE (0.005U), followed by addition of EDC and incubation for 5min to form a semi-stable compound, then NHS was added and incubated for 10min to immobilize AChE onto the GQDs. Hg2+ at 50μM was added into the GQDs system to quench the fluorescence. Then ATCh, as the substrate of AChE, was added to recover the quenched fluorescence. After 10min, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. The fluorescence recovery ratio of the GQDs was proportional to the AChE activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of inhibitors on AChE activity
ce:section.ce:para: Two traditional AChE inhibitors, paraoxon and tacrine, were employed to verify the feasibility of AChE inhibitor screening. Before the addition of ATCh, different doses of the inhibitors were added into the quenched GQD system. After 10min of incubation, the emission spectra were recorded.

The UV–vis absorption and emission spectra were examined for GQDs and AChE-immobilized GQDs. The excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs were confirmed to be 387nm and 457nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2a, after addition of EDC/NHS and immobilization of AChE, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was enhanced by about 20 times, and the spectra showed almost no change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of EDC/NHS dose on fluorescence enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence enhancement effect by EDC/NHS is shown in Fig. 2b. Varying doses of EDC/NHS (0.5–5mM) were added into MES-Tris buffer containing GQDs. The emission intensity increased with growing concentrations of EDC/NHS. It could be seen that a concentration of 2.5mM resulted in the maximum level of fluorescence enhancement. However, the enhancement effect decreased with concentrations beyond 2.5mM. It could be concluded that an excessive dose of EDC/NHS led to weakening of the enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for the AChE assay

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence enhancement of GQD concentration is shown in Fig. 2c. Varying concentrations of GQDs (2.5–25μgmL−1) were added into the buffer containing EDC/NHS (2.5mM). A good linearity illustrating GQD concentration to fluorescence intensity was obtained. As a result, a GQD concentration of 5μgmL−1 was used in the following tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching effect of Hg2+ on the enhanced GQD fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Different doses of Hg2+ (0–100μM) were added into the GQD system. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence quenching rate was proportional to the Hg2+ concentration and a logarithm curve was obtained. Finally, 50μM was chosen as the quenching concentration. As reported, Hg2+ has a stronger affinity than other metal ions towards the carboxylic groups on the GQD surface [31]. The selective and strong quenching was presumably due to electron or energy transfer from the GQDs to Hg2+ as suggested in the literature [32].

The undiluted GQD solution was employed to determine the quenching effect of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 3b, the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of the Hg2+ quenching rate was 75.17μM, which was higher than the value of 15.44μM presented in Fig. 3a. It could be concluded that the addition of EDC/NHS obviously reduced the detection limitation of Hg2+, which resulted in the reduced use of ATCh, thus increasing the sensitivity of inhibitor screening. Because of the enhanced fluorescence intensity, a lower dose of GQDs could reach the same fluorescence intensity; thus, the IC50 of Hg2+ was decreased.

After the quenching, ATCh was mixed with the Hg2+-GQDs system. The fluorescence recovery based on the substrate concentration is shown in Fig. 4 . The results showed a gradual recovery of the GQD emission with increasing ATCh concentrations. It could be concluded that the thiol compounds released by enzyme hydrolysis could bind to Hg2+ through Hg-S bonding interactions, which competed with the GQDs for the binding with Hg2+, resulting in recovery of the quenched fluorescence [29]. Depending on the amount of ATCh added to the assay solution, certain amounts of Hg2+ were removed from the surface of the GQDs. As shown in the inset, the fluorescence recovery value was plotted against the ATCh concentration, and a logarithmic relationship was observed in the range of 0–100μM ATCh. It can be seen that along with the increasing ATCh concentration, fluorescence recovery ratio increased quickly. However, the increasing speed of fluorescence recovery ratio began to slow down over the concentration of 40μM. The corresponding fluorescence recovery ratios of 50 and 100μM concentration are nearly the same, indicating no more fluorescence can be recovered beyond 50μM. Since the optimized dosage of Hg2+ is 50μM, it could be concluded that the reaction ratio of ATCh to Hg2+ was nearly 1:1, thus 50μM was chosen to be the optimal ATCh concentration.

The activity of AChE was determined based on the fluorescence quenching of the GQD system under optimal conditions. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence spectra of GQDs in the presence of a fixed concentration of ATCh and different concentrations of AChE. The result indicates that the increased intensity in the quenched fluorescence was correlated to the increase in the amount of AChE. The result suggests that the fluorescence quenching performance was caused by thiocholine, the product of the catalytic hydrolysis of ATCh. With the increase in the amount of AChE in the system, the production of thiocholine was also promoted, in turn causing the thiocholine to react with the Hg2+-GQDs via the thiol group. GQDs were released via the dissociation of Hg2+-GQDs, thus the quenched fluorescence could be recovered in accordance with the dose of thiol groups. Under optimal conditions, the quenched fluorescence intensity increased with increasing AChE concentration (10−5 to 10−2 U). The limit of detection for AChE was 2.3×10−6 U. From curves 8 and 9 in Figs. 4 and 5, no obvious changes in the fluorescent intensity of GQDs were observed since there were no thiocholine products in the presence of only ATCh or AChE. Moreover, the role of the thiol group in the quenching of our system was further confirmed by substituting the acetylthiocholine substrate with acetylcholine, which did not result in any changes in the fluorescence spectra of the GQDs.

When AChE inhibitors were added into the GQD reaction mixture, the fluorescence recovery level decreased relative to the dose of the inhibitors. The hydrolysis rate of ATCh induced by AChE was slowed down upon the addition of AChE inhibitors. Accordingly, we evaluated the inhibitory activity of the inhibitors using the GQD system. The concentration of paraoxon added into the GQD system was in the range of 0–500nM, whereas that of tacrine was 0–60nM. As shown in Fig. 6 , two logarithmic curves illustrating the ratio of inhibitor concentration to inhibitory activity were obtained, suggesting the feasibility of the system. The IC50 of paraoxon and tacrine were determined to be 63.76 and 14.07nM, respectively. These results are lower than those reported in the literature of about 5.5μM and 70nM [20], suggesting that this assay has a higher sensitivity and a lower detection limit for AChE inhibitors.

A highly sensitive GQD-based optical nanosensor for the simple fluorescence turn-on detection of AChE activity and turn-off detection of AChE inhibitors has been developed. The sensing strategy was rooted in the quenching of the enhanced fluorescence of GQDs by Hg2+, the dissociation of the Hg2+-GQDs complex, and the recovery of the fluorescence upon the production of sulfhydryl compounds by the AChE reaction. The assay offers a convenient and rapid approach for AChE inhibitor detection and potential screening with higher sensitivity. The developed method may be of potential use for the assay of other enzyme which catalyzes substrate to release thiol compounds.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21275098), Doctor Base Foundation of Chinese Ministry of Education (20110202110005).

Graphene quantum dots as sensor for phenols in olive oil
Control the size and surface chemistry of graphene for the rising fluorescent materials
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
Optically tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Aryl-modified graphene quantum dots with enhanced photoluminescence and improved pH tolerance
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots and its application to the determination of uric acid
Interfacing water soluble nanomaterials with fluorescence chemosensing: graphene quantum dot to detect Hg2+ in 100% aqueous solution
One-pot synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for Fe3+ ions detection
Modulated dye retention for the signal-on fluorometric determination of acetylcholinesterase inhibitor
Affinity binding-guided fluorescent nanobiosensor for acetylcholinesterase inhibitors via distance modulation between the fluorophore and metallic nanoparticle
An update on pharmacological approaches to neurodegenerative diseases
Controlled immobilization of acetylcholinesterase on improved hydrophobic gold nanoparticle/Prussian blue modified surface for ultra-trace organophosphate pesticide detection
An amperometric biosensor for acetylcholine determination prepared from acetylcholinesterase-choline oxidase immobilized in polypyrrole-polyvinylsulpfonate film
Continuous fluorometric assays for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibition with conjugated polyelectrolytes
Self assembly of acetylcholinesterase on a gold nanoparticles-graphene nanosheet hybrid for organophosphate pesticide detection using polyelectrolyte as a linker
Assembly of carbon nanotubes on a nanoporous gold electrode for acetylcholinesterase biosensor design
A new and rapid colorimetric determination of acetylcholinesterase activity
Fluorescent graphene quantum dot nanoprobes for the sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions
A graphene quantum dot-based method for the highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn on detection of biothiols
Covalent immobilization of proteins on carbon nanotubes using the cross-linker 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide-a critical assessment
L-cysteine functionalized gold nanoparticles for the colorimetric detection of Hg2+ induced by ultraviolet light
Glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots as selective fluorescent probes for phosphate-containing metabolites

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tian He received his BSc (2007) and MSc (2011) degrees in major of applied chemistry from school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Xi’an Shiyou University. He is currently a PhD candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University. His research interests include enzyme engineering and protease assay.

Liang Qi received his bachelor degree in pharmaceutical engineering from school of pharmaceutical sciences, Shandong University of Traditional Chinese Medicine in 2011. He is a PhD candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University. His research interest is bioanalytical chemistry.

Jing Zhang is studying her PhD degree at School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering and an associate professor in the institute of exercise physiology in Shaanxi Normal University. Her main research fields involve the analytical chemistry or sport nutrition and biochemistry.

Ya-Li Huang received her BSc (2014) degree in major of chemistry from school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Shaanxi Normal University. She is currently a chemistry teacher of Zhanjiang No.1 middle school.

Zhi-Qi Zhang is a professor in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Shaanxi Normal University. He received his doctor degree (analytical chemistry) from college of chemistry & materials science, Northwest University in 2004. His current research interests include bioanalytical chemistry, pharmaceutical analysis, and new materials for separation and enrichment.
